motion.rationale: CSS animations are present and intentional throughout—text splitting with stagger, slideUp/scaleIn animations with calculated delays. Canvas elements are declared but no addFrameTask or procedural drawing code is visible. Motion exists but doesn't carry meaning beyond entrance choreography.
motion.evidence: split="word" with animation-delay: calc(var(--ef-word-index) * 100ms); canvas elements like <canvas id="hero-canvas"> are declared but no rendering code shown; animations are entrance-only (slideUp, scaleIn, fadeOut)

specificity.rationale: Uses Supabase's actual positioning ('open source Firebase alternative'), mentions real features (PostgreSQL, Row Level Security, JSONB), and the green/slate color palette approximates brand. But the structure—hero, problem, feature, feature, CTA—is generic SaaS template. Any backend-as-a-service could use this exact flow.
specificity.evidence: "Supabase is the open source Firebase alternative"; PostgreSQL code snippet; mentions of RLS, MFA, magic links. But 'Stitching together a dozen services' problem statement applies equally to Firebase, PlanetScale, Neon, etc.

viewer_state.rationale: Follows formulaic arc: intrigue (hero) → frustration (problem) → relief (solution) → trust (features) → action (CTA). Each transition is predictable. The 'problem' scene attempts emotional shift but 'dozen services' is abstract rather than visceral.
viewer_state.evidence: Scene 2 labeled 'The Problem' with generic pain ('separate bill, separate config, separate vendor lock-in')—this is telling not showing. Scenes 3-4 are feature dumps with identical structure (badge → title → description → visual).

point_of_view.rationale: The 'open source' angle hints at a perspective (transparency, ownership, anti-lock-in), but it's stated rather than embodied. The composition doesn't commit to why open source matters differently here. Structure is standard product demo template.
point_of_view.evidence: Title says 'Open Source Database Made Simple' but nothing in the visual or structural language demonstrates openness—no code transparency, no community, no self-hosting angle explored. Badges like 'THE FOUNDATION' and 'BUILT-IN' are generic category labels.

omission.rationale: Five scenes for 45s is reasonable density. Scenes 3 and 4 (Database/Auth) are structurally identical—both could potentially merge. The composition is cut off mid-Scene 5, so full assessment is incomplete, but visible scenes each advance the narrative.
omission.evidence: Scenes 3 and 4 share exact pattern: badge animation → split-word title → description text → visual element. Different content but identical form suggests possible consolidation. Scene 5 truncated at 'animation: 0.8s fadeOut var(--ef-transition-out-'

feedback.observation: The problem scene ('Stitching together a dozen services') and solution structure could describe any integrated backend platform. No uniquely Supabase truth shapes the narrative.
feedback.improvement: Lean into what makes Supabase specifically different: show the actual Postgres SQL console, demonstrate that your data isn't abstracted away, contrast with Firebase's proprietary query language, or show the open-source GitHub repo as proof of transparency.

feedback.observation: Scenes 3 and 4 produce the same emotional state—'oh, this feature exists'—with no escalation or contrast between them. Feature listing doesn't build momentum.
feedback.improvement: Create tension between scenes: show a developer struggling with auth complexity THEN reveal the one-line Supabase solution. Or demonstrate how RLS eliminates entire categories of security bugs. The viewer should feel relief or surprise, not just receive information.

feedback.observation: The composition claims 'open source' matters but never shows why. The perspective is announced, not argued through form.
feedback.improvement: If open source is the point of view, make it visible: show the GitHub stars, display the actual source code, contrast vendor lock-in with 'you own your data.' Or commit to a different angle entirely—speed, developer experience, cost—and let the structure prove it.

feedback.observation: Canvas elements are declared but appear to have no procedural content. The stagger animations are well-timed but decorative rather than semantic.
feedback.improvement: Use the canvas elements: animate a database schema forming in real-time, show data flowing between tables, visualize the auth token lifecycle. Motion should demonstrate what Supabase does, not just make text entrances prettier.
